name: CERN Concerns
description: CERN Concerns encompass a range of conspiracy theories and anxieties surrounding the European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN), the world's largest particle physics laboratory. Primarily based in Switzerland and France, CERN operates the Large Hadron Collider (LHC), a massive machine used to study the fundamental constituents of matter. Concerns range from fears that the LHC could create black holes that would engulf the Earth, to claims that CERN is opening portals to other dimensions or manipulating reality in dangerous ways. These theories often draw on misinterpretations of scientific concepts, anxieties about technological advancement, and distrust of large institutions. Proponents often point to unusual weather patterns, strange noises reported near CERN, and perceived esoteric symbolism within the organization's logo or activities as evidence. Mainstream scientific perspectives overwhelmingly dismiss these concerns, citing rigorous safety protocols, peer-reviewed research demonstrating the impossibility of LHC-created black holes posing a threat, and the lack of any credible evidence supporting claims of dimensional portals or reality manipulation. Concerns have circulated extensively online, fueled by social media and alternative news sources, and often intersect with broader anxieties about globalization, scientific authority, and potential apocalyptic scenarios. The widespread dissemination of these fears highlights the importance of scientific literacy and critical thinking in evaluating complex scientific endeavors.
subtitle: Fears and conspiracy theories surrounding CERN

claims.claim: CERN will create a black hole that will destroy the Earth.
claims.evidence: Speculation about the LHC's energy levels and potential for creating microscopic black holes.
claims.criticism: Scientific consensus is that any black holes created would be microscopic and evaporate almost instantly, posing no threat to Earth. Calculations and experiments have repeatedly confirmed this.

claims.claim: CERN is opening portals to other dimensions.
claims.evidence: Reports of unusual weather phenomena near CERN, perceived symbolism in CERN's logo and ceremonies, and interpretations of quantum physics.
claims.criticism: Weather phenomena are unrelated to CERN, perceived symbolism is subjective and lacks scientific basis, and interpretations of quantum physics are often misapplied and lack experimental support. No credible evidence of dimensional portals exists.

claims.claim: CERN is manipulating reality and altering timelines.
claims.evidence: Subjective experiences of Mandela Effects (false memories), perceived inconsistencies in current events, and interpretations of CERN's experiments as attempts to control the universe.
claims.criticism: Mandela Effects are explained by psychological phenomena such as false memories and suggestibility. There is no scientific basis for claiming CERN can alter timelines or manipulate reality. These claims are pseudoscientific.

keyFigures.name: Sergio Bertolucci
keyFigures.role: Former Director for Research and Computing at CERN, often cited by conspiracy theorists.

keyFigures.name: Brian Cox
keyFigures.role: Physicist and science communicator, frequently debunks CERN conspiracy theories.

mythologyConnections: Babylonian Tower of Babel (ambition and hubris)

timeline.event: Circulation and proliferation of CERN conspiracy theories online.

sources.author: John Ellis
sources.work: Safe at Any Energy? An Assessment of the LHC Safety Case
sources.title: John Ellis
